0_1_1.title: Balcony Site Prep & Soil Test
0_1_1.subtitle: Measuring tape, Trowel · 45 min
0_1_1.why: Understanding unknown sunlight and drainage on your balcony is crucial for lettuce health, and preparing your existing soil proactively addresses unmeasured nutrients on a low budget.
0_1_1.how: Over the next day, observe your 25 sqft balcony for sun exposure patterns. Mix your current soil (which has a good pH 6.5) with available kitchen scraps (e.g., coffee grounds, crushed eggshells) to enrich it, then ensure all your containers have sufficient drainage holes for successful growth.
0_1_1.target: Documented average daily sunlight hours (min 4-6 hrs), visible drainage holes in all containers, and enriched potting mix prepared.
0_1_1.tools_required: Measuring tape, Trowel, Containers, Kitchen scraps

0_1_1.title: Lettuce Seed Sowing
0_1_1.subtitle: Seed packets, Trowel · 20 min
0_1_1.why: Precise sowing ensures good germination, especially since your soil temperature of 22°C is at the upper limit for lettuce, requiring careful attention to depth to avoid heat stress for young seedlings.
0_1_1.how: Fill your chosen containers with the prepared potting mix. Sow lettuce seeds thinly, about 0.5 cm deep, ensuring even distribution across your balcony space. Gently cover the seeds with a thin layer of soil and water immediately using a fine spray to avoid disturbing them.
0_1_1.target: 80% germination rate within 5-7 days with evenly distributed seedlings.
0_1_1.tools_required: Lettuce seeds, Containers, Trowel, Watering can (fine spray)

0_1_1.title: Watering & Thinning Seedlings
0_1_1.subtitle: Small watering can, Gloves · 30 min
0_1_1.why: While your current moisture is 65% (optimal for lettuce), consistent moisture within the 60-80% range is vital for young plants, and thinning prevents overcrowding in your limited balcony space, ensuring robust growth for your critical delivery.
0_1_1.how: Check soil moisture daily by inserting a finger; water gently if the top inch feels dry, aiming to keep moisture levels stable. Once seedlings have a few true leaves, thin them to 10-15 cm apart by carefully pinching off weaker plants at the soil line, allowing the stronger ones to thrive.
0_1_1.target: Soil moisture consistently between 60-80%, seedlings spaced 10-15 cm apart.

0_1_1.title: Low-Budget Nutrient Boost
0_1_1.subtitle: Large jar, Strainer, Watering can · 25 min
0_1_1.why: With unmeasured soil nutrients and a low budget, a homemade liquid feed provides essential support for your growing lettuce, complementing your current optimal pH of 6.5.
0_1_1.how: Prepare a 'compost tea' by soaking vegetable scraps (e.g., banana peels, diluted coffee grounds) in water for 2-3 days in a large jar. Strain the liquid, then dilute it further (1 part tea to 5-10 parts water) before watering your lettuce plants, delivering a gentle nutrient boost.
0_1_1.target: Visibly healthier, darker green lettuce leaves within 3-5 days after application.
0_1_1.tools_required: Large jar, Vegetable scraps, Strainer, Watering can

0_1_1.title: Pest Check & Bolting Prevention
0_1_1.subtitle: Gloves, Hand lens (optional) · 20 min
0_1_1.why: Regular checks prevent pest damage and bolting, which is crucial as your soil temperature of 22°C is at the upper end of optimal, potentially triggering premature flowering and affecting the yield for Greenwood Public School.
0_1_1.how: Thoroughly inspect the undersides of leaves for any signs of pests like aphids, slugs, or leaf miners, removing them manually or with a gentle water spray. If you notice plants stretching or developing a central stem, move pots to a slightly shadier spot on your balcony during the hottest part of the day to prevent bolting.
0_1_1.target: No significant pest infestations, no signs of bolting (e.g., stem elongation or bitter taste).

0_1_1.title: Final Moisture & Harvest Plan
0_1_1.subtitle: Watering can, Scissors · 15 min
0_1_1.why: Maintaining optimal soil moisture (60-80%) in the final days is crucial for ensuring the crisp texture and quality of your lettuce before harvest, which is critical for your 3.0 kg yield target and the Greenwood Public School delivery.
0_1_1.how: Perform a final soil moisture check; if it feels dry, water thoroughly but avoid overwatering to prevent root rot. Plan your harvest strategy: either 'cut-and-come-again' by taking outer leaves or 'full head' harvest, considering the critical priority of your delivery hub.
0_1_1.target: Soil moisture consistently between 70-80%, harvest method clearly decided.

0_1_1.title: Harvest Lettuce for Delivery
0_1_1.subtitle: Sharp knife/scissors, Basket · 30 min
0_1_1.why: Timely and proper harvest maximizes your expected 3.0 kg yield and ensures the freshest quality for the critical delivery to Greenwood Public School, completing your 28-day cycle successfully.
0_1_1.how: Harvest your lettuce in the early morning for best flavor and crispness. If cutting full heads, slice at the base of the plant with a sharp knife or scissors. For 'cut-and-come-again', snip outer leaves, allowing the inner leaves to continue growing for future harvests. Rinse gently and prepare for immediate transport.
0_1_1.target: Minimum 3.0 kg of fresh, clean lettuce harvested and ready for delivery.
0_1_1.tools_required: Sharp knife/scissors, Harvesting basket/container, Cleaning cloth

102_4_3.title: Adjust Soil pH
102_4_3.subtitle: Soil pH meter, Tiller · 45 min
102_4_3.why: Your current soil pH of 7.0 is slightly alkaline for carrots, which prefer a range of 6.0–6.8 for optimal nutrient absorption and healthy root development.
102_4_3.how: Use your advanced soil pH meter to confirm the reading. Incorporate elemental sulfur or sphagnum peat moss into the top 6-8 inches of soil using a tiller, carefully following product guidelines to gently lower the pH.
102_4_3.tools_required: Soil pH meter, Tiller/Rotary hoe, Elemental sulfur OR Sphagnum peat moss

102_4_3.title: Sow Carrot Seeds
102_4_3.subtitle: Hand trowel, Moisture meter · 60 min
102_4_3.why: Consistent moisture is crucial for carrot germination, but your current soil moisture of 72.0% is at the higher end, requiring careful watering to prevent waterlogging and rot.
102_4_3.how: Create shallow furrows (1/4 to 1/2 inch deep) in your 66.7 sqft community plot. Sow seeds thinly, then gently cover with fine soil. Use your moisture meter and drip irrigation to maintain soil moisture between 60-70% for germination.
102_4_3.target: Even germination observed within 7-10 days, soil moisture 60-70%.
102_4_3.tools_required: Hand trowel, Moisture meter, Carrot seeds, Drip irrigation kit

102_4_3.title: Thin Seedlings & Weed
102_4_3.subtitle: Gardening gloves, Hand weeder · 30 min
102_4_3.why: Thinning is vital in your 66.7 sqft community plot to give each carrot sufficient space for proper root development and prevent competition from weeds for resources.
102_4_3.how: Once seedlings are 2-3 inches tall and show true leaves, carefully thin them to stand 2-4 inches apart, removing the weakest. Gently remove any weeds around the delicate carrot plants by hand or with a precision weeding tool.
102_4_3.target: Carrot plants spaced 2-4 inches apart; plot visibly weed-free.

102_4_3.title: Assess Soil Nutrients
102_4_3.subtitle: Advanced soil test kit · 20 min
102_4_3.why: Since nutrient levels are unknown, a mid-cycle assessment with your high budget and advanced tools is crucial to ensure optimal phosphorus and potassium levels for healthy carrot root development.
102_4_3.how: Collect several soil samples from different areas of your plot. Use your advanced soil testing kit to analyze levels of key nutrients like N, P, K. Based on results, plan for targeted fertilization if deficiencies are identified.
102_4_3.target: Nutrient levels analyzed, action plan for fertilization documented if needed.
102_4_3.tools_required: Advanced soil testing kit, Sample bags/containers

102_4_3.title: Hill Carrots & Monitor Moisture
102_4_3.subtitle: Hand trowel, Moisture meter · 25 min
102_4_3.why: Hilling protects carrot 'shoulders' from sun exposure, preventing greening and bitterness, while continuous moisture monitoring is important as your initial soil moisture was high (72.0%).
102_4_3.how: Gently draw loose soil up around the crowns of developing carrots to cover any exposed tops. Continue using your moisture meter to maintain soil moisture consistently between 50-70%, avoiding both waterlogging and drought.
102_4_3.target: No green carrot shoulders visible; soil moisture consistently within 50-70%.

102_4_3.title: Scout for Pests & Diseases
102_4_3.subtitle: Hand lens, IPM supplies · 30 min
102_4_3.why: Early detection and intervention against pests or diseases are critical to safeguard your expected 131.5 kg yield, especially in a shared community plot where issues can spread.
102_4_3.how: Systematically inspect carrot leaves, stems, and roots for signs of pests (e.g., rust fly larvae, aphids) or disease (e.g., leaf blight). Utilize your advanced tools and high budget for immediate organic intervention if any issues are found.
102_4_3.target: Plot free from significant pest infestations or disease outbreaks.
102_4_3.tools_required: Hand lens, Organic pest control spray (e.g., neem oil), Sticky traps

102_4_3.title: Prepare for Harvest
102_4_3.subtitle: Hand fork, Harvesting bins · 40 min
102_4_3.why: Confirming carrot maturity and preparing for careful harvest ensures the best quality and presentation for your standard priority delivery to the North Community Centre.
102_4_3.how: Gently loosen soil around a few carrots with a hand fork to check their size and readiness. Water the plot thoroughly 12-24 hours before your planned harvest date to ease extraction. Prepare clean harvesting bins for transport.
102_4_3.target: Carrots visually confirmed mature and ready for harvest; clean harvesting bins prepared.
102_4_3.tools_required: Hand fork/broadfork, Harvesting bins/crates, Watering can/hose

102_0_3.title: Soil Prep & Planting
102_0_3.subtitle: Advanced soil tester, trowel · 30 min
102_0_3.why: Your current pH of 7.0 is at the upper limit for optimal tomato growth; a slight adjustment now can optimize nutrient uptake for vigorous initial development.
102_0_3.how: Use your advanced soil tester to confirm the soil pH. If it remains at 7.0, incorporate a small amount of peat moss or elemental sulfur into the top 6 inches of soil using a trowel to gently lower it. Then, plant tomato seedlings or seeds according to spacing recommendations for your 66.7 sqft plot, ensuring adequate depth.
102_0_3.target: Soil pH adjusted to 6.5-6.8; 100% of seedlings successfully planted.
102_0_3.tools_required: Advanced soil tester, Trowel, Tomato seeds/seedlings, Peat moss/Elemental sulfur

102_0_3.title: Install Supports & Monitor
102_0_3.subtitle: Tomato stakes/cages, plant ties · 45 min
102_0_3.why: Early support is crucial to prevent your tomato plants from sprawling, ensuring proper air circulation and maximizing light exposure given your unknown sunlight conditions and to manage space on your community plot.
102_0_3.how: As plants reach 6-8 inches tall, install sturdy stakes or cages, utilizing your advanced tools for efficient setup around each plant. Gently tie the main stem to the support using soft ties, leaving room for growth. Re-check your soil moisture (currently 72.0%) and temperature (20.0°C) with your advanced tester to ensure they remain within optimal ranges.
102_0_3.target: All plants securely staked; soil moisture 60-80%, temperature 18-27°C.
102_0_3.tools_required: Tomato stakes/cages, Plant ties, Advanced soil tester

102_0_3.title: First Fertilization
102_0_3.subtitle: Liquid tomato fertilizer, watering can · 20 min
102_0_3.why: Since nutrient levels are currently unmeasured, providing a balanced, initial feeding is essential to support the vigorous vegetative growth phase of tomatoes, targeting your high expected yield of 166.8 kg.
102_0_3.how: Choose a high-quality, balanced liquid fertilizer (e.g., 5-10-5 or similar) appropriate for tomatoes, leveraging your high budget. Dilute the fertilizer according to package instructions for young plants. Apply it evenly around the base of each plant, ensuring it reaches the root zone without directly contacting the stem.
102_0_3.target: Plants show visible signs of healthy, robust growth (e.g., increased leaf production, thicker stems) within 7 days.

102_0_3.title: Pruning & Health Check
102_0_3.subtitle: Pruning shears, gardening gloves · 30 min
102_0_3.why: Pruning suckers improves air circulation and directs plant energy to fruit production, which is vital for achieving your 166.8 kg yield target, especially on a shared community plot where space and light are premium.
102_0_3.how: Carefully inspect each plant for suckers, which are shoots growing in the crotch between the main stem and a leaf branch. Using clean, sharp pruning shears, snip off suckers close to the main stem. While pruning, thoroughly check for any signs of pests or diseases on leaves and stems, especially given the unknown sunlight and drainage conditions.
102_0_3.target: 70-80% of suckers removed; no visible signs of pests or diseases on inspected plants.

102_0_3.title: Second Fertilization & Env Check
102_0_3.subtitle: Flowering/fruiting fertilizer, advanced soil tester · 25 min
102_0_3.why: A second nutrient boost is critical for fruit development and to maintain plant health throughout the ripening stage, directly supporting your high yield goal while ensuring your soil parameters remain optimal for your specific crop conditions.
102_0_3.how: Apply a liquid fertilizer, this time with a slightly higher phosphorus and potassium content to encourage flowering and fruiting, diluted as per instructions. After application, re-check your soil pH (aiming for 6.0-7.0), moisture (60-80%), and temperature (18-27°C) using your advanced soil tester to ensure optimal conditions persist for fruit set.
102_0_3.target: Visible increase in flower production; soil parameters maintained within optimal ranges for tomato (pH 6.0-7.0, moisture 60-80%, temp 18-27°C).
102_0_3.tools_required: Flowering/Fruiting liquid fertilizer, Watering can, Advanced soil tester

102_0_3.title: Fruit Development Support
102_0_3.subtitle: Watering can, soft plant ties · 30 min
102_0_3.why: Consistent watering and additional support are essential as your plants develop fruit, preventing issues like blossom end rot and ensuring successful ripening for your expected harvest.
102_0_3.how: Monitor soil moisture closely daily; although currently 72.0%, fruit development demands consistent water. Water deeply and regularly, especially given your unknown water availability, to maintain moisture between 70-80%. Add additional ties to stakes as fruit clusters develop to support the increased weight, preventing branches from breaking under the load.
102_0_3.target: Soil moisture consistently maintained between 70-80%; no branches breaking under fruit weight; no signs of blossom end rot.

102_0_3.title: Prepare for Harvest
102_0_3.subtitle: Harvesting basket, gardening gloves · 20 min
102_0_3.why: As the plants approach the end of their 70-day cycle, preparing for a phased harvest ensures you capture your expected 166.8 kg yield efficiently for the North Community Centre delivery hub.
102_0_3.how: Inspect ripening fruit daily, looking for even color and firmness typical of a ripe tomato. Gather appropriate harvesting tools such as a sturdy basket or bucket and gardening gloves. Clear any immediate debris or overgrowth around the plants to facilitate easy access for picking, ensuring gentle handling of the produce to avoid bruising.
102_0_3.target: First batch of ripe tomatoes identified and ready for gentle picking within the next 2 days.

102_1_3.title: Site Assessment & Initial Prep
102_1_3.subtitle: Shovel, Rake, Light Meter · 1 hour
102_1_3.why: Even with optimal initial soil conditions (pH 7.0, moisture 72.0%, temp 20.0°C), understanding your plot's sunlight exposure and drainage is crucial for preventing future issues in your community garden.
102_1_3.how: Use an advanced light meter to log sun exposure hourly for a full day across your 66.7 sqft plot. Dig a small test pit to observe water percolation and drainage efficiency. Clear any lingering debris or old plant matter from the area.
102_1_3.target: Documented sunlight hours and drainage observation for the 66.7 sqft plot.

102_1_3.title: Soil Nutrient Testing
102_1_3.subtitle: Soil Probe, Sample Bags · 45 min
102_1_3.why: Your soil nutrient levels are currently not measured, which is a critical gap for your high-budget farm aiming for a 100.1 kg yield and needing to optimize conditions beyond the existing pH 7.0 and moisture 72.0%.
102_1_3.how: Collect 5-7 soil samples from various locations within your 66.7 sqft plot using a soil probe, mixing them into a composite sample. Send this sample to a professional lab for comprehensive NPK, micronutrient, and organic matter analysis to inform precise fertilization plans.
102_1_3.target: Completed soil nutrient report, outlining NPK and micronutrient levels.
102_1_3.tools_required: Soil probe, Sample bags, Lab testing kit

102_1_3.title: Planting & Initial Watering
102_1_3.subtitle: Trowel, Drip Irrigation · 2 hours
102_1_3.why: Planting lettuce seedlings efficiently leverages your farm's current optimal soil moisture of 72.0% and temperature of 20.0°C, ensuring a strong start for the 28-day grow cycle.
102_1_3.how: Carefully space lettuce seedlings 8-12 inches apart within your 66.7 sqft plot, accommodating the specific variety for maximum yield. Install and activate your drip irrigation system to provide immediate, gentle watering, settling the soil around the transplanted roots.
102_1_3.target: All seedlings planted and uniformly watered; initial setup of drip irrigation confirmed.
102_1_3.tools_required: Trowel, Lettuce seedlings, Drip irrigation system

102_1_3.title: Monitor & Thin Seedlings
102_1_3.subtitle: Small Shears · 1 hour
102_1_3.why: Thinning ensures optimal spacing and resource distribution for each lettuce plant within your 66.7 sqft community plot, crucial for developing robust plants given your excellent starting conditions of pH 7.0, moisture 72.0%, and temp 20.0°C.
102_1_3.how: Regularly inspect your seedlings for healthy germination and growth uniformity. Once seedlings have developed their first true leaves, gently thin them to their final desired spacing (e.g., 8-10 inches apart), removing the weaker or overcrowded plants to prevent competition.
102_1_3.target: Seedlings thinned to optimal spacing (e.g., 8-10 inches) across the entire plot.

102_1_3.title: Nutrient Application
102_1_3.subtitle: Fertigation System, Soil Report · 1 hour
102_1_3.why: Applying targeted nutrients based on your soil test results is vital for boosting lettuce growth and achieving your ambitious 100.1 kg yield goal, complementing your stable initial soil readings of pH 7.0, moisture 72.0%, and temp 20.0°C.
102_1_3.how: Based on your comprehensive soil nutrient report, prepare a balanced liquid fertilizer or compost tea appropriate for lettuce. Use your advanced fertigation system to precisely deliver the nutrient solution directly to the root zones, ensuring even distribution across the 66.7 sqft plot.
102_1_3.target: Nutrient solution applied uniformly as per soil test recommendations; plants show signs of healthy growth.
102_1_3.tools_required: Fertigation system, Soil nutrient report, Liquid fertilizer

102_1_3.title: Continuous Monitoring & Patrol
102_1_3.subtitle: Soil Sensor Network, Hand Lens · 30 min
102_1_3.why: Regularly checking your soil's pH (currently 7.0), moisture (72.0%), and temperature (20.0°C) with advanced tools helps maintain optimal conditions, while early pest detection protects your crop and ensures yield for the North Community Centre.
102_1_3.how: Utilize your advanced soil sensor network to continuously monitor and log pH, moisture, and temperature fluctuations. Conduct visual inspections of all plants, using a hand lens for close examination to identify any signs of pests, diseases, or nutrient deficiencies promptly.
102_1_3.target: Soil parameters maintained within optimal lettuce range; any pests/diseases identified and initial remediation planned.
102_1_3.tools_required: Soil sensor network, Hand lens, Integrated pest management (IPM) supplies

102_1_3.title: Final Watering & Harvest Prep
102_1_3.subtitle: Moisture Meter, Harvesting Knife · 45 min
102_1_3.why: Ensuring optimal moisture (maintaining near 72.0%) just before harvest is key to maximizing lettuce crispness and weight, directly contributing to your 100.1 kg yield goal and preparing for the North Community Centre delivery.
102_1_3.how: Monitor soil moisture closely with your advanced meter, aiming to keep it in the 70-80% range up until 1-2 days before the planned harvest, then reduce watering slightly. Clean and sharpen all harvesting knives and prepare appropriate crates for collecting the lettuce.
102_1_3.target: Soil moisture maintained for optimal plant turgidity; all harvesting tools and containers ready.
102_1_3.tools_required: Soil moisture meter, Harvesting knife, Crates/bins

102_1_3.title: Harvest & Delivery Logistics
102_1_3.subtitle: Harvesting Knife, Crates · 3 hours
102_1_3.why: Efficient harvesting and immediate cooling are crucial steps to preserve the quality and freshness of your lettuce for the North Community Centre, directly impacting the final 100.1 kg yield and its marketability.
102_1_3.how: Harvest lettuce early in the morning by cutting the base of each head cleanly with a sharp knife. Immediately transport the harvested heads to a shaded area for initial cleaning and then into a cooling station (if available) or onto ice to reduce field heat before packing for delivery to the North Community Centre.
102_1_3.target: 100.1 kg of fresh lettuce harvested, cleaned, and cooled for delivery.
102_1_3.tools_required: Harvesting knife, Crates/bins, Cooling station (or ice)

102_7_3.title: Prepare Microgreen Trays
102_7_3.subtitle: Seedling Trays & Spreader · 45 min
102_7_3.why: Setting up your specialized trays properly on your 66.7 sqft community plot ensures optimal germination space and efficient use of resources from your high budget for your Microgreens Cycle #3.
102_7_3.how: Clean and sanitize your advanced seedling trays. Prepare a soilless growing medium (e.g., coco coir or peat moss) in each tray, leveling it to about 1 inch deep. Moisten the medium lightly to your current moisture level of 72%, which is within the optimal 60-80% range for microgreens.
102_7_3.target: 10-15 prepared trays with moistened growing medium ready for seeding.
102_7_3.tools_required: Seedling trays,Soilless growing medium,Leveling tool/Spreader,Watering can

102_7_3.title: Sow Microgreen Seeds
102_7_3.subtitle: Seed Dispenser & Mister · 30 min
102_7_3.why: Precise sowing is crucial for uniform growth and yield (expected 33.4 kg) of your microgreens, utilizing your advanced tools to ensure even seed distribution and initial hydration.
102_7_3.how: Evenly spread microgreen seeds densely across the surface of the prepared trays using your seed dispenser. Gently mist the seeds with water until the top layer of medium is thoroughly moist but not waterlogged, maintaining your optimal soil moisture between 60-80%. Cover the trays immediately to maintain darkness and humidity for germination.
102_7_3.target: All prepared trays uniformly seeded and covered.

102_7_3.title: Monitor Germination & Humidity
102_7_3.subtitle: Hygrometer & Moisture Meter · 15 min
102_7_3.why: Consistent humidity and moisture are vital for successful germination of microgreens, especially with your soil moisture at 72.0% and ambient humidity at 68.0%, both within optimal ranges.
102_7_3.how: Briefly lift covers to inspect for initial signs of sprouting. Ensure the growing medium remains consistently moist; if dry spots appear, lightly mist with water. Use your advanced hygrometer to confirm humidity under covers is high, and your moisture meter to keep the medium within the 60-80% optimal range.
102_7_3.target: Visible sprouts in at least 50% of trays, medium consistently moist.

102_7_3.title: Introduce Light & Airflow
102_7_3.subtitle: Grow Lights & Fan · 25 min
102_7_3.why: As sprouts emerge, introducing appropriate light and airflow is essential for strong stem development and preventing mold, especially since your ambient humidity is 68.0%.
102_7_3.how: Once most seeds have germinated and developed initial leaves, remove tray covers. Position your advanced grow lights (due to unknown sunlight) approximately 4-6 inches above the seedlings for 12-16 hours daily. Use a small fan to provide gentle air circulation, preventing damping-off and maintaining healthy growth.
102_7_3.target: Seedlings growing upright, vibrant green, and no signs of mold.

102_7_3.title: Water & pH Check
102_7_3.subtitle: pH Meter & Watering Can · 20 min
102_7_3.why: Maintaining consistent moisture and verifying pH is critical for the healthy growth of your microgreens, as your current soil pH of 7.0 is at the upper end of the optimal 6.0–7.0 range, and your soil temperature of 20.0°C is perfectly optimal.
102_7_3.how: Check the moisture levels in each tray daily using your moisture meter, watering gently from the bottom or top with a fine mist as needed to keep it within the 60-80% optimal range. Periodically use your advanced pH meter to check the runoff water from a few trays; if it significantly deviates below 6.0, consider adjusting your water pH for subsequent waterings. Your current soil temperature of 20.0°C is ideal for growth.
102_7_3.target: Growing medium moisture consistently 60-80%, pH of runoff water between 6.0-7.0.
102_7_3.tools_required: Soil moisture meter,Watering can/Bottom watering trays,pH meter

102_7_3.title: Prep for Harvest & Spot Check
102_7_3.subtitle: Inspection Magnifier · 30 min
102_7_3.why: Preparing for harvest ensures peak quality for your expected 33.4 kg yield and allows a final check for pests or diseases, maximizing your high-budget investment in quality control.
102_7_3.how: Examine your microgreens closely using an inspection magnifier for any signs of pests or disease, ensuring they are clean and healthy. Confirm that plants have developed their first true leaves and reached the desired height and leaf development for harvesting. Prepare your harvesting tools by sterilizing them.
102_7_3.target: Microgreens are pest-free, healthy, and uniformly ready for harvest; harvesting tools are clean.

102_7_3.title: Harvest & Pack Microgreens
102_7_3.subtitle: Harvest Shears & Containers · 90 min
102_7_3.why: Timely and efficient harvesting at the end of your 14-day cycle is crucial to achieve the expected 33.4 kg yield and maintain quality for delivery to North Community Centre.
102_7_3.how: Using sharp, sterile harvest shears or a knife, carefully cut the microgreens just above the soil line, ensuring minimal disturbance to the roots. Gently gather the harvested greens and immediately package them into clean, food-grade containers. Label each container for delivery to the North Community Centre.
102_7_3.target: All mature microgreens harvested, packaged, and ready for transport, aiming for the 33.4 kg yield.
102_7_3.tools_required: Harvest shears/knife,Food-grade containers,Labels,Scale (optional for yield tracking)

102_2_3.title: Plot Prep & Planting Spinach
102_2_3.subtitle: Soil aerator, precision seeder · 90 min
102_2_3.why: Your soil pH 7.0 is perfectly within spinach's optimal range (6.0-7.5) and moisture at 72.0% is ideal for germination, but unknown drainage should be addressed through proper preparation.
102_2_3.how: Use your advanced tools for precise soil aeration and furrow creation across the 66.7 sqft community plot. Plant spinach seeds 0.5 inches deep and 2-3 inches apart in rows 12-18 inches apart, ensuring even distribution for optimal growth.
102_2_3.target: Evenly planted spinach seeds across the 66.7 sqft plot, with improved soil structure.

102_2_3.title: Germination & Initial Water
102_2_3.subtitle: Moisture meter, watering can · 30 min
102_2_3.why: Your current soil temperature of 20.0°C is at the upper end of spinach's optimal range, which can accelerate germination but also increase initial water demand.
102_2_3.how: Daily, gently check the soil surface for emerging seedlings and ensure consistent moisture. Use your moisture meter to confirm soil moisture remains between 55-75% and apply light watering with a fine spray if needed to avoid disturbing seeds.
102_2_3.target: At least 70% of planted seeds show signs of germination, with soil moisture maintained at 65-70%.
102_2_3.tools_required: Moisture meter, watering can with fine rose

102_2_3.title: Thinning Spinach Seedlings
102_2_3.subtitle: Hand pruners, gloves · 60 min
102_2_3.why: Thinning is crucial for your community plot's 66.7 sqft to prevent overcrowding, which can lead to stunted growth and lower yield from this Cycle #3.
102_2_3.how: Once seedlings develop 2-3 true leaves, carefully thin them to stand 4-6 inches apart using small hand tools. Prioritize removing weaker or diseased seedlings to promote robust growth of remaining plants.
102_2_3.target: Spinach plants are spaced 4-6 inches apart, with strong, healthy individual plants.

102_2_3.title: Mid-Cycle Nutrient Boost
102_2_3.subtitle: Watering can, liquid fertilizer · 45 min
102_2_3.why: With 'nutrients not measured' and spinach being a heavy feeder, a mid-cycle boost is vital to support the expected 80.0 kg yield from your 66.7 sqft plot.
102_2_3.how: Given your high budget and advanced tools, conduct a quick soil nutrient test if possible to inform your decision. Apply a balanced liquid organic fertilizer (e.g., fish emulsion or seaweed extract) diluted as per instructions directly to the base of the plants.
102_2_3.target: Spinach leaves show vibrant green color and vigorous growth, indicating nutrient uptake.
102_2_3.tools_required: Soil test kit (optional), watering can, liquid organic fertilizer

102_2_3.title: Pest/Disease Scout & Check
102_2_3.subtitle: Magnifying glass, organic spray · 45 min
102_2_3.why: Unknown sunlight, drainage, and water availability conditions on your community plot necessitate proactive pest and disease management, especially with your current soil humidity at 68.0%.
102_2_3.how: Systematically inspect plants for signs of pests or disease, paying close attention to leaf undersides and new growth. If issues are found, apply organic pest control methods and adjust watering practices as needed.
102_2_3.target: No significant pest infestations or disease symptoms observed on spinach plants.
102_2_3.tools_required: Magnifying glass, organic pest spray, gloves

102_2_3.title: Final Moisture Adjustment
102_2_3.subtitle: Moisture meter, watering system · 30 min
102_2_3.why: Maintaining soil moisture within the optimal 55–75% range is critical for tender leaf development as harvest approaches, especially considering your initial moisture of 72.0%.
102_2_3.how: Use your advanced moisture meter to precisely check soil moisture levels across the plot. Adjust watering to ensure the soil is consistently moist but not waterlogged, reducing watering slightly a day or two before harvest to concentrate flavors.
102_2_3.target: Soil moisture consistently between 60-70%; plants appear plump and healthy for harvest.
102_2_3.tools_required: Moisture meter, watering system (if automated), harvesting crates

102_2_3.title: Harvest & Delivery Prep
102_2_3.subtitle: Harvesting shears, harvest bins · 120 min
102_2_3.why: Prompt and efficient harvesting is key to achieving your 80.0 kg yield target from this Cycle #3 and ensuring fresh delivery to the North Community Centre.
102_2_3.how: Using clean shears or a sharp knife, harvest outer leaves first or cut the entire plant at the base, leaving 1 inch to allow for regrowth if desired. Immediately transfer harvested spinach into appropriate containers for transport to the North Community Centre.
102_2_3.target: At least 80% of mature spinach harvested from the 66.7 sqft plot and prepared for delivery.
